text: This is a technical guide on robotic external iliac deep inguinal and obturator lymph node dissection.
This is a forty-five years old male with stage three anorectal melanoma with right inguinal lymph node metastasis.
His initial symptoms include tinnitus and anal pain for a few months.
After his staging scans, he then went on to have neoadjuvant immunotherapy consisting of nivolumab and ipilimumab.
On the third cycle of immunotherapy, he had concurrent focal anorectal radiotherapy as well.

From the initial evaluation using flexible endoscopy, you can tell that this patient has an extensive disease involving the exterior of the anal canal, extending throughout the anal canal and into his rectum.
This was further confirmed by the MRI of the pelvis, which showed that this tumor extended approximately six centimeters from the anorectal junction.
After his neoadjuvant treatment, we restaged his disease, and the CT showed a persistent superficial inguinal nerve node and suspicious external iliac nerve nodes.
Therefore, the operative steps were robotic abdominal perineal resection,

The robotic surgical system is redocked to perform dissection of the external iliac, deep inguinal, and obturator lymph nodes, with the robotic arms maneuvering precisely around these anatomical structures.
As for the positioning, the patient was positioned in a tridimervous position with access to the right groin and the pictogram on the right side shows the proposed placement for the robotic approach.

The surgeon identifies the ureter, obturator nerve, umbilical artery, and vas deferens in preparation for the lymph node dissection, with these anatomical structures highlighted in the surgical field.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the external iliac vessels, exposing the vas deferens beneath.
The surgeon continues the incision lateral to the obliterated umbilical artery, further exposing the surgical field for the ileorectal anastomosis procedure.
The surgeon returns to the lateral side of the ureter, using sharp dissection to separate the areolar tissue surrounding the ureter.
The surgical team gains access to deeper tissue layers following the incision and dissection of surrounding anatomical structures, including the ureter and vas deferens.
The surgeon uses a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to expose and identify the deep portion of the obliterated umbilical artery.
The surgeon continues to expose the vas deferens by carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue, revealing more of the structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon returns to the lateral side of the obliterated umbilical artery and incises the entire extent of the peritoneum, revealing deeper anatomical layers.
The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery, vas deferens, and ureter, creating a clear visual roadmap for the external iliac nerve node dissection.
The surgeon exposes the deep inguinal ring and obturator lymph node packet, revealing these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects down to the adventitia of the external iliac artery, carefully separating the surrounding tissue to expose the artery's outermost layer.
The surgeon picks up the areolar tissue overlying the external iliac artery and makes an incision straight down to the adventitia of the artery, exposing the underlying structure.
The surgeon sharply incises the areolar tissue, progressively exposing the proximal part of the external iliac artery, revealing its anatomical course.
The surgeon performs dissection on the lateral side of the lymph node packet adjacent to the psoas muscle, continuing the exposure and separation of anatomical structures.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the deep inguinal ring, focusing on the area surrounding the external iliac artery and adjacent lymph node packets.
The surgeon identifies a nerve structure adjacent to the lymph node packet and carefully preserves it by pushing it away from the area of interest.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the deep inguinal ring, carefully separating the tissue from the surface of the psoas muscle.
The surgeon carefully removes the lymph node packet, which has been dissected free from surrounding structures, including the psoas muscle and external iliac artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the external iliac artery, identifying an overlying vessel which is cauterized before proceeding with sharp dissection.
The surgeon returns to the proximal part of the lymph node packet, skeletonizing a feeding vessel. A hemi-lock is applied to the vessel before excising it using CoTRI.
The surgeon mobilizes the lymph node packet located beneath the external iliac artery, continuing dissection to free the packet from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses forceps to grasp the lymph node packet and move it to the opposite side, facilitating further dissection and mobilization.
After dissecting the lymph node packet from the proximal part of the artery and muscle, the surgeon initiates a deep inguinal lymph node dissection, focusing on the deeper anatomical structures.
The surgeon begins dissecting the lymph node packet from the distal portion of the external iliac artery, carefully separating the tissue from the artery.
The surgeon performs blunt and sharp dissection to carefully progress towards the deep inguinal ring, continuing the maneuver to mobilize the lymph node packet.
The surgeon aims to connect the current dissection plane with the previously established plane from the superficial inguinal lymph node dissection.
The surgeon identifies the most distal extent of the dissection by locating the surgical clip placed during the open procedure.
The surgeon dissects the lymph node packet from the external iliac vein, moving medially while carefully separating it from the underlying muscle and artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects the lymph node packet from the external iliac vein while approaching the deep inguinal ring. A small vein is visible above the external iliac vein during this process.
The surgeon marks the extent of the dissection above the external iliac vein, indicating the completion of the lymph node packet removal in this area.
The surgeon continues the dissection into the deeper portion, carefully separating tissue from the external iliac vein, revealing the muscle belly beneath.
The surgeon continues the dissection beneath the small vein, carefully separating areolar tissue to identify the clip placed during the open procedure.
The surgeon performs an obturator lymph node dissection, carefully removing lymphatic tissue from the obturator region.
The surgeon begins dissecting the lymph node packet along the entire length of the external iliac vein, carefully separating it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon applies adequate counter traction to expose the thin external adiabane, allowing for sharp incision of the areola tissue using cautery.

The surgeon returns to the distal portion of the lymph node packet near the obturator muscle, carefully dissecting from medial to lateral. The obturator nerve is identified, lying medial to the dissected lymph node packet.
The surgeon exposes the obturator artery, revealing it alongside adjacent structures. The dissection proceeds proximally, carefully separating the artery from the surrounding anatomical structures.

The surgeon returns to the proximal extent of the lymph node packet, carefully dissecting it off the internal iliac artery. The dissection proceeds into the deeper portion, where the proximal portion of the obturator nerve is identified.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection near the obturator nerve to avoid nerve stimulation, carefully separating the lymph node packet from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the obturator dissection by carefully separating the lymph node packet from the obturator nerve, ensuring the nerve is fully exposed and free from surrounding tissue.

The final histopathology showed an anorectal melanoma with extensive tumor regression and twenty-two negative lymph nodes. The only positive lymph nodes was identified in the superficial inguinal lymph node region, where there is one out of twelve.
The external iliac, the inguinal and obturator lymph nodes had fifty negative lymph nodes, but there were aggregates of melanophages with non-viable tumors identified.
That concludes our technical guide.
